tagline: I build and ship systems across Go, Rust, and TypeScript — including a live, deployed SaaS platform. Hands-on with auth, async job pipelines, distributed rate limiting, and storage engine internals.
about: I'm a self-directed backend and systems engineer based in Nigeria. I build the kind of projects you'd encounter in production — authentication systems, persistence layers, distributed workflows, probabilistic data structures, and developer tooling.

My primary languages are Go, Rust, and TypeScript. I care about how systems fail, how they recover, and how engineers on-call understand what's happening. Structured logging, request IDs, and latency tracking are not afterthoughts in my code.
Actively seeking backend, systems, or infrastructure roles — internship or entry-level, remote or international.

projects.name: Redir
projects.desc: A production-shaped media upload and CDN redirect service that shows what a real Go backend looks like internally. The interesting engineering is in the details: presigned URLs are generated once and reused from cache to avoid hammering S3; the auth stack covers bcrypt, Redis-backed sessions, email verification, Google and GitHub OAuth, and product-scoped API keys — all in one service. A companion Go SDK handles resumable batch uploads with stable sequence IDs to prevent duplicates on retry. Integration tests cover the full auth lifecycle across a real DB and Redis.

projects.name: Bolt
projects.desc: An async job processing service built to understand what reliable background work actually requires. Redis Streams gives durable, ordered delivery with at-least-once semantics — if a consumer crashes mid-pipeline, the job isn't lost. Each job runs a three-step pipeline: PDF invoice generation, outbound webhook delivery to third-party systems, and transactional email dispatch. PostgreSQL tracks job state so failures are observable and retryable, not silent.

projects.name: ShipFlow
projects.desc: A live, deployed collaboration platform — the only project here you can open in a browser right now. The interesting architecture decision was extracting high-throughput webhook ingestion into a Cloudflare Worker (Hono + D1 + Drizzle + CF Queues) rather than letting it block the main backend, with shared event contracts and idempotency keys keeping both sides consistent. The backend has been through a full JS → TS rewrite and is currently being ported to Go for better performance and explicit error handling.

projects.name: CrispyDB
projects.desc: A storage engine built from scratch to understand how databases actually persist data. The core problem: reads should never touch disk unnecessarily. The solution is a two-stage read path — check the in-memory BTreeMap first, then consult per-file Bloom filter indices (xxh3_128) before deserializing any .bin files. False positives cost one wasted disk read; misses are impossible by design. Tombstone-based deletes and a shutdown manifest mean the engine restarts into a consistent state. The roadmap targets LSM-style layout and compaction.

projects.name: Rate Limiter
projects.desc: A distributed token bucket library where the interesting constraint was correctness under concurrency. The Redis backend uses Lua scripting to make the check-and-decrement operation atomic — without that, two requests racing on the same key can both pass a limit that should only allow one. The in-memory backend uses Moka cache for high-throughput local use. A shared trait lets callers swap backends without changing call sites. Returns structured decisions (allow / retry_after / remaining) with Criterion benchmarks to back the performance claims.

projects.name: ShardedMap
projects.desc: A concurrent map built specifically to understand the tradeoffs in lock contention at scale. The insight: a single Mutex<HashMap> serializes all writes regardless of which keys are involved. Sharding by key hash means threads only contend when they touch the same shard. Benchmarked against DashMap and a naive global-lock baseline with Criterion across 1–16 threads — achieved ~2.3x higher throughput than the global-lock baseline at 16 threads. Over-sharding adds ~22% overhead at low contention with no concurrency to offset it, confirming shard count is a tunable, not a default.

projects.name: watch
projects.desc: A file watcher and command runner that solves the everyday problem of manually restarting a process after every change. The interesting constraint was making it language-agnostic — watch doesn't know or care what your command does, it just monitors a directory recursively, filters by extension, debounces noisy events, and restarts the process cleanly using process-group management so child processes don't linger. Supports both TOML and JSON config with conflict detection at startup if both exist.

alsoBuilding.name: Queues
alsoBuilding.desc: multi-group job queue with claim/ack semantics, O(1) doubly-linked list backed by HashMap, DashMap for thread-safe group management

alsoBuilding.name: dnsgo
alsoBuilding.desc: dual-mode DNS server with authoritative zone support and recursive resolver with full delegation chain walking

availability: Open to backend, systems, or infrastructure roles — internship or entry-level, remote or international.
